findings:
## analyst-research Report — GPU in VS Code Projects
### Sprint: gpu-vscode-projects-2026-03-05

## Top Findings

**1. VS Code Monaco WebGPU Renderer** (github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/221145)
- Tech: WebGPU — WGSL vertex+fragment shaders, texture atlas, double-buffering
- Purpose: Zero-latency Monaco text rendering; shaders run parallel to CPU layout
- Implementation: 3000×200 pre-allocated GPU buffer (7.2MB). Glyph atlas rasterized on CPU via canvas-2D, uploaded to GPU. Scroll via uniform offset (no per-frame upload). Branch: tyriar/gpu_exploration.
- CSP: VS Code core, no VSIX sandbox applies. Electron Chromium WebGPU direct.
- Date: Jul 2024–ongoing. Still Backlog milestone. NOT yet in stable releases.

**2. AI Toolkit (ms-windows-ai-studio) + Foundry Local** (marketplace.visualstudio.com, Sep 2025)
- Tech: ONNX Runtime / DirectML (Windows) / Metal (Mac) / CUDA (Linux) + WinML NPU for Copilot+ PCs
- Purpose: Local GPU/NPU LLM inference (Phi-4, Qwen Coder) as GitHub Copilot model provider
- Implementation: Extension manages catalog+download UI. Inference in native Foundry Local sidecar over localhost HTTP. No GPU inside VSIX. DirectML abstracts NVIDIA/AMD/Intel.
- CSP: Standard VSIX. Sidecar out-of-sandbox.
- Date: Sep 2025 launch, Mar 2026 articles confirm active use.

**3. GitHub Copilot BYOM + Ollama/LM Studio** (Oct 2025)
- Tech: llama.cpp CUDA/Metal via Ollama or LM Studio, serving OpenAI-compatible API
- Purpose: Privacy-preserving GPU LLM in GitHub Copilot via BYOM model provider config
- Implementation: User sets GPU layers in LM Studio (n_gpu_layers) or Ollama auto-detects CUDA. Extension adds localhost endpoint as Copilot provider. Zero GPU in extension.
- Date: Oct 2025 (peterfalkingham.com), active through Mar 2026

**4. Continue.dev** (marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=Continue.continue)
- Tech: HTTP proxy to Ollama/LM Studio/Foundry Local GPU backends
- Purpose: 2.26M installs. Open-source (Apache 2.0) AI coding agent with chat/edit/autocomplete/agent.
- Implementation: React webview for chat UI (nonce-CSP, asWebviewUri). Extension sends FIM/chat requests to configured GPU server over HTTP.
- Date: Active Jan-Mar 2026

**5. OHZI GLB Viewer** (Three.js/WebGL in VS Code webview, Jun 2025)
- Tech: Three.js → WebGL hardware-accelerated in vscode.WebviewPanel
- URL: marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=OHZIInteractiveStudio.ohzi-vscode-glb-viewer
- Purpose: Preview GLB 3D models in editor (DRACO, interactive, hierarchy tree, wireframe/normals)
- Implementation: Bundle Three.js in VSIX. localResourceRoots → extension media/. nonce CSP. Works on Windows/Mac via Electron Chromium WebGL. Linux: hardware-specific issues reported.
- Date: Updated Jun 2, 2025

**6. Nogic — Codebase Graph Visualization** (Jan 2026)
- URL: marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=Nogic.nogic
- Tech: Webview graph renderer (likely Canvas/WebGL force-directed graph)
- Purpose: Visualize codebase structure as interactive graph. 4.8k installs, 4.3/5 rating.
- Notes: Performance issues: 5-10 FPS on moderate codebases; crashes on 2000+ files. Unoptimized GPU usage. Initially closed-source — now on Open VSX.
- Date: HN Show Jan 13, VSM article Jan 22, 2026

**7. Jupyter Extension** (101.9M installs — dominant GPU ML pattern)
- Tech: Python kernel subprocess with full CUDA access (PyTorch, TF, JAX)
- Purpose: Notebook-based GPU ML. Kernel has unrestricted GPU access per OS drivers.
- Implementation: VS Code Notebook API + external Python kernel process. Outputs rendered as MIME via sub-extensions (Plotly, Vega). Zero GPU in extension itself.
- Date: Ongoing, primary GPU-ML pattern in VS Code

**8. ML.NET WebView ONNX Pattern** (proposed Nov 2025)
- URL: github.com/dotnet/machinelearning/issues/7545
- Tech: ONNX Runtime Web (WebGL/WebGPU backend) in VS Code webview
- Purpose: In-browser ML inference without external process. Still feature-request stage.
- Blocker: SharedArrayBuffer/COEP header requirements in VS Code webview security model unresolved.

## Patterns Discovered

1. **External-Process GPU** (dominant): All LLM extensions proxy to native sidecar (Ollama, Foundry Local, LM Studio). Extension is pure HTTP coordinator. Required by VSIX constraints (no CUDA native addons).
2. **Three.js/WebGL in Webview**: Bundle Three.js into VSIX → localResourceRoots → nonce-CSP → WebGL canvas. Works on Windows/Mac. Linux unreliable.
3. **VS Code Core WebGPU** (non-extension): Monaco WebGPU renderer uses WGSL shaders directly in Electron frame. Not in extension sandbox.
4. **Python Kernel CUDA Bridge**: Jupyter pattern — external Python process owns GPU, extension renders MIME outputs.
5. **NPU/DirectML (Windows-emerging)**: AI Toolkit introduces CPU/GPU/NPU model picker. Copilot+ PCs. DirectML as cross-vendor abstraction.

## Gaps / Open Questions

1. Is navigator.gpu available inside VS Code extension webviews (vs core editor frame)? No public extension has shipped WebGPU in webview. Untested.
2. SharedArrayBuffer/COEP in VS Code webviews: required for ONNX Runtime Web WASM. Webview isolation policy may block this.
3. Native CUDA .node addons in VSIX: theoretically possible but ABI + driver version mismatch makes it impractical. No extension has shipped this.
4. WebGPU compute shaders (not render) in any VS Code extension webview: zero evidence of any attempt.
5. VS Code 1.96-1.99 GPU release notes: no GPU rendering features shipped to stable in this window. Monaco WebGPU is still pre-release.
recommendations: Use External-Process GPU pattern: route LLM inference via Ollama/Foundry Local sidecar over localhost HTTP — the only proven shippable VSIX pattern.
Three.js/WebGL in webview is viable for 3D/graph viz: bundle into VSIX, use localResourceRoots + nonce CSP. Test Linux separately (WebGL may need flags).
Before designing any WebGPU feature in an extension webview: run a targeted navigator.gpu availability test across VS Code 1.96-1.110 — this is currently undocumented.
Investigate SharedArrayBuffer/COEP support in VS Code webviews before attempting ONNX Runtime Web in-webview inference (required for WASM threading).
Target DirectML/ONNX as the Windows GPU abstraction layer (abstracts CUDA+AMD+Intel); avoid CUDA-specific native addons in VSIX packaging.
